text: We describe a case of combined robotic liver and rectal resection for an adenocarcinoma of the high rectum with a synchronous liver metastasis. The patient is a seventy-two years old male presenting with rectal bleeding and anemia. The clinical assessment reveals an adenocarcinoma of the high rectum with a three centimeter synchronous liver metastasis located between S seven and S eight and classified as CT three N zero M one.
After a multidisciplinary team discussion, a neoadjuvant chemotherapy with Fofox was started. The restaging after three moths of chemotherapy showed a partial response according to resist criteria.
The UMARCS score showed an high surgical difficulty for the rectal resection. A combined liver wedge resection and rectal resection with partial mesorectal excision was planned.

The procedure begins with hepatic resection using the da Vinci robotic system. The iRobotic card is positioned on the patient's left side, while the assistant surgeon stands between the patient's legs. A pneumoperitoneum is established through a five-centimeter incision with an Alexis retractor. Four eight-millimeter robotic trocars and a twelve-millimeter laparoscopic trocar are inserted. The hepatogastric ligament is opened, and a Foley catheter is placed around the hepatic pedicle.

The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound to locate and demarcate the resection margins of a three-centimeter lesion in the right hepatic lobe. The ultrasound shows the lesion has no relationship with the principal portal pedicles and hepatic veins. The coronary ligament is cut, and an atypical hepatectomy of segments seven and eight is initiated under ultrasound guidance.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using a monopolar hook, bipolar forceps, and a vessel sealer. Hemlock clips are applied to the major portal and hepatic veins to achieve hemostasis.

The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using two intermittent intracorporeal Pringle maneuvers, each lasting fifteen minutes. During the hepatic resection, two main subsegmental portal pedicles and three hepatic veins are identified, clipped, and sectioned.

After achieving hemostasis, the surgical specimen is extracted through the fan and steel incision. The procedure continues with the rectal resection, involving repositioning robotic ports with a twelve millimeter and eight millimeter trocar. The robotic boom is adjusted, and a new target anatomy is established for an extended medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon dissects the area respecting the Girauda fascia, identifying the left ureter. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected 1.5 cm from the aorta, clipped with Hemolock, and sectioned. The inferior mesenteric vein undergoes similar dissection, clipping, and sectioning. The dissection continues until the lower border of the pancreas is visible. An extended medial-to-lateral approach is used for splenic flexure mobilization, separating the superior surface of the pancreas from the retroperitoneum and opening the lesser sac.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon by dissecting the Toldt fascia using monopolar scissors and a vessel sealer, separating the colon from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon begins the PME by dissecting the posterior plane, followed by the right and left planes. The tumor is visible on the anterior surface of the hyrectum, and the mesorectum dissection is performed five centimeters distal to the rectal lesion.
The surgeon completes the procedure by performing a stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, followed by the creation of a diverting ileostomy at the site of robotic port number two.

Overall operative time was three hundred ten minutes. The postoperative period was uneventful. The patient was discharged at postoperative day seven. The histological exam, showed an R-zero resection of an PT-III and one AM-I adenocarcinoma, with one out of fifteen positive lymph nodes, and, a R-zero resection of a three-centimeter colorectal liver metastasis.
